stories.headline: Boulder firebombing suspect sentenced to life plus 2,128 years after pleading guilty to all state charges
stories.summary:
Mohamed Sabry Soliman, 46, pleaded guilty Thursday to all state charges stemming from the June 2025 firebombing attack on a group of protesters on Pearl Street in Boulder. Judge Nancy W. Salomone sentenced Soliman to life in prison without the possibility of parole, plus an additional 2,128 years — the maximum sentence for each of the 101 substantive counts, which included murder, attempted murder, first-degree assault and possession of an incendiary device.

Soliman threw Molotov cocktails at demonstrators who were peacefully protesting for the release of Israeli hostages on June 1, 2025. More than 20 victims were identified, roughly half of whom were physically injured. One victim, 82-year-old Karen Diamond, later died of her injuries. Soliman faces separate federal charges arising from the same attack.

During sentencing, Soliman spoke for more than 30 minutes, offering condolences and an apology before delivering an anti-Zionist message and asking prosecutors to impose the death penalty. Boulder District Attorney Michael Dougherty called Soliman's actions 'cowardly' and said each victim deserved justice. Multiple victims delivered impact statements describing lasting trauma and heightened anxiety in their daily lives.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers pass bill banning 'surveillance pricing'; Polis veto threat looms
stories.summary:
The Colorado Senate passed House Bill 1210 on Wednesday on a 19-15 vote, sending legislation that would ban companies from using consumer-data algorithms to set individualized prices to Gov. Jared Polis' desk. If signed, the measure would prohibit businesses from using personal data to set prices on goods such as groceries, plane tickets and electronics, and would block gig platforms like Uber and Lyft from using such data to set individualized worker wages.

Polis has offered a cold reception to the bill, with his office declining to confirm whether he will sign it. A spokesman referenced an earlier statement in which Polis expressed concern about 'policies that further interfere with the free functioning of markets.' Polis vetoed a similar measure last year targeting rent-setting algorithms.

Supporters, including the Colorado Restaurant Association and the Bell Policy Center, argue the bill would protect consumers from price discrimination. Opponents, led by the Colorado Technology Association and banking industry groups, contend the measure is overly broad and would disrupt routine business practices. If signed, Colorado's law would be considered the strongest surveillance-pricing restriction in the country.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers pass credit card swipe fee bill exempting sales tax; heads to Polis
stories.summary:
The Colorado House passed Senate Bill 134 on Wednesday by a 44-20 vote, advancing legislation that would remove sales taxes from the calculation of credit card interchange fees charged to merchants. The bill now heads to Gov. Polis for his signature. Under current practice, merchants pay swipe fees calculated as a percentage of the total transaction, including sales tax; the bill would base fees only on the underlying purchase price.

Supporters, including the Colorado Restaurant Association and House Speaker Julie McCluskie, argue the measure will save small businesses thousands of dollars annually without raising consumer prices. The restaurant and retail industries have lined up behind the bill, calling it meaningful financial relief.

The banking industry has fought the legislation vigorously, arguing that isolating sales tax from swipe fees in a single state — representing roughly 2% of national transactions — would be technically and financially burdensome. The Colorado Bankers Association warned that banks might decline to alter their payment systems, creating confusion for Colorado merchants and consumers. A similar Illinois law is currently entangled in litigation, which opponents cite as a cautionary precedent.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis signs law cracking down on critical infrastructure theft, targeting copper wire thieves
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed House Bill 26-1101 into law, establishing new requirements aimed at curtailing the theft of copper wire and telecommunications materials from cell towers, electric substations and other critical infrastructure across Colorado. The law requires recycling yards to maintain detailed records of purchases and report suspicious sales involving telecom infrastructure materials, with criminal penalties for businesses that fail to comply.

Lawmakers behind the bill said copper theft has become a growing problem statewide, capable of knocking out cell service, causing power outages and leaving exposed electrical wires that create public safety hazards. Rep. Matt Soper, a sponsor of the bill, noted that rural Coloradans are especially vulnerable because they depend heavily on cell service for emergencies.

Under the new law, thieves could face enhanced charges tied specifically to critical infrastructure theft, with penalties scaled to the value of the stolen materials. Polis said the law will help protect the infrastructure that keeps 'trains running, homes heated and buildings safe.'

stories.headline: Denver launches small business loan program of up to $350,000 funded by marijuana sales tax revenue
stories.summary:
Denver's Economic Development and Opportunity Department announced this week that it is partnering with the Colorado Enterprise Fund to offer low-interest small business loans ranging from $10,000 to $350,000, funded through a city account that draws 1% of retail marijuana sales tax revenues. The Denver City Council approved the three-year program, which will make up to $9 million in loan capital available at a subsidized interest rate of 4.99%.

The program honors former National Black Chamber of Commerce Chair Herman Malone and is designed to serve undercapitalized businesses. Applicants will also receive coaching assistance throughout the life of their loans, and the city will offer guidance to help small business owners complete the application process.

Applications open June 1. The Colorado Enterprise Fund, which has distributed $442 million in small business capital over its 50-year history, will administer the loans as a revolving fund, meaning repaid dollars will be reinvested to support future entrepreneurs. Virtual information sessions are scheduled for June 9 and June 25.

stories.headline: Gas prices spike to $4.42 per gallon average in metro Denver, straining commuters and rideshare drivers
stories.summary:
The average price of regular gasoline in metro Denver reached $4.42 per gallon as of Monday, according to GasBuddy, a jump of 68 cents from the prior month and $1.49 higher than the same time last year. The statewide average hit $4.40 per gallon, up $1.26 from a year ago, while diesel averaged $5.50 per gallon across Colorado — $2.11 more than last year.

The price surge is pushing some Front Range commuters to reconsider their living and work arrangements. Ben Brink, a Longmont resident who commutes to Cherry Hills Village, said his monthly gas bill nearly doubled to $1,200, prompting him to leave his job and start a business closer to home. Rideshare drivers and long-distance commuters with limited alternatives, including families traveling to Denver for medical appointments, are also bearing the brunt of the increases.

GasBuddy petroleum analyst Patrick De Haan said gasoline prices rose in every state over the past week and that broader price volatility is likely to persist in the near term. Prices varied widely across the state, with Glenwood Springs and Vail averaging $4.71 per gallon while Greeley and Pueblo saw lower averages near $4.34 to $4.36.

stories.headline: Upscale Denver noodle shop Reckless Noodle House permanently closes after three years
stories.summary:
Reckless Noodle House, an Asian fusion noodle shop and cocktail bar at 820 Sherman St. in Denver, announced this week that it has permanently closed. The restaurant, which opened approximately three years ago, served Vietnamese noodle bowls, Chinese duck, fried rice and papaya salad, and was brought to Denver from Seattle by owners who had operated a sister location there for five years.

'After much consideration, we've made the difficult decision to permanently close our restaurant and bar in Denver. Sometimes you have to know when to say when,' the owners wrote on the restaurant's website. They thanked staff and guests for their loyalty over the years, describing the establishment as 'a place of connection, celebration, and belonging.'

The closure adds to a string of Denver restaurant departures in recent months. The owners did not specify the reasons for shutting down but indicated the decision was not made lightly.

stories.headline: U-Haul truck crashes through wall of Denver Uptown restaurant; same owners' other eatery recently hit by fire
stories.summary:
A U-Haul truck crashed through the west wall of Ollie & Park's, an American-style tapas restaurant at 1210 E. 17th Ave. in Denver's Uptown neighborhood, on Sunday afternoon around 4 p.m. The truck penetrated approximately 10 feet into the restaurant, demolishing the bar and striking a structural support pillar for apartments above. No customers or staff were injured, as the bartender had stepped away from the bar at the time of the crash.

Owners Hallie and Jason Alfonso said they were at a park with their children when they received the call. The cause of the accident has not been determined; the Alfonsos expect to receive a Denver Police Department report within days and estimate repair costs could exceed $100,000. The restaurant, which opened in 2024, will remain closed for an extended period.

The incident is the second major shock for the couple this year. Their other Denver restaurant, Odyssey Italian Restaurant & Wine Bar at 603 E. 6th Ave., sustained fire damage in February. Odyssey is expected to reopen in three to four weeks, while Ollie & Park's faces a longer recovery timeline.

stories.headline: CU Denver faculty raise conflict-of-interest concerns over chancellor's relationship with faculty director
stories.summary:
The Faculty Assembly Executive Committee at the University of Colorado Denver sent a formal letter to CU System President Todd Saliman in April expressing concerns about Chancellor Ken Christensen's relationship with Bryn Harris, a tenured school psychology professor who also serves as director of the Center for Faculty Development and Advancement. The CFDA oversees resources that influence faculty career advancement, including tenure and grant opportunities.

Christensen disclosed the relationship through proper university channels in late 2025, and CU Vice President of Communications Michele Ames said a confidential mitigation plan was put in place shortly after, walling off the chancellor from CFDA-related decisions. Ames said both individuals are consenting adults who followed reporting rules.

The faculty committee wrote back this week saying that keeping the mitigation plan confidential does little to address the real or perceived conflicts of interest it is designed to manage. President Saliman responded that the plan ensures appropriate separation, but faculty said transparency is needed to restore confidence. Christensen was appointed CU Denver chancellor in February 2025.

stories.headline: Metro Denver residents directed to debris drop-off sites following snowstorm tree damage
stories.summary:
Communities across the Denver metro area are working to clean up extensive tree damage left by last week's late spring snowstorm, which snapped large branches across neighborhoods and brought down power lines in some areas. Residents in Arvada and other communities described waking to the sound of crashing limbs and discovering downed lines in their yards.

Local governments are opening and extending hours at debris collection sites to help residents dispose of broken branches and other storm debris. Broomfield's Tree Branch Recycling Facility extended its hours through May 8, accepting materials from noon to 5 p.m., with proof of Broomfield residency required. Additional drop-off locations are being compiled by local outlets as more municipalities announce options.

The storm delivered heavy, wet snow that proved particularly damaging to trees that had already leafed out for spring, adding weight to branches not yet hardened for winter conditions. Cleanup is expected to continue across the region for several days.

stories.headline: Cale Makar named Norris Trophy finalist for sixth straight season, matching Bobby Orr's historic mark
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche defenseman Cale Makar was officially named a finalist for the James Norris Memorial Trophy on Thursday, marking the sixth consecutive season he has earned the distinction. The feat makes Makar only the second defenseman in NHL history to accomplish the feat, joining Boston Bruins legend Bobby Orr.

Makar, a two-time Norris Trophy winner, is joined in this year's finalist field by Columbus Blue Jackets defenseman Zach Werenski and Buffalo Sabres star Rasmus Dahlin. The announcement came on a day when Makar was not available to speak with reporters after taking a maintenance day at the Avalanche's practice facility in Centennial.

Avalanche teammates were quick to praise Makar's sustained excellence. Center Nazem Kadri said he was 'not surprised at all' by the recognition, pointing to Makar's consistency as the most impressive aspect of his game. 'This guy does it every single night,' Kadri said. The Avalanche are currently in the Stanley Cup Playoffs after eliminating the Minnesota Wild in the first round.

stories.headline: Broncos agree to rookie contracts with TE Justin Joly and S Miles Scott ahead of minicamp
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have finalized standard four-year rookie contracts with fifth-round tight end Justin Joly and seventh-round safety Miles Scott, the Denver Post reported Thursday. The deals add to previously completed contracts for seventh-round tight end Dallen Bentley and seventh-round linebacker Red Murdock, bringing the number of Broncos draft picks under contract to four ahead of rookie minicamp, which runs Friday through Sunday.

Joly, selected 152nd overall, is set to receive a four-year, $4.9 million deal with a $514,996 signing bonus. Scott, who began his college career at Illinois as a wide receiver before converting to safety, will receive a four-year, $4.51 million deal with a $125,444 signing bonus. The team's top three picks — third-round defensive lineman Tyler Onyedim, fourth-round running back Jonah Coleman and fourth-round offensive lineman Kage Casey — will participate in minicamp on injury protection deals while contract negotiations continue.

Joly is viewed as one of the more intriguing rookies in the class, projecting as a natural pass-catching tight end who will initially back up veteran Evan Engram. The Broncos are also inviting 33 players on a tryout basis for minicamp, including Dutch kicker/punter Paul Geelen of Northern Illinois, former Colorado offensive lineman Zarian McGill and Colorado State defensive back Jahari Rodgers.

joke.joke: My trainer told me to do lunges. That's a big step forward for my fitness.

weekendEvents.title: RiNo Street Fair
weekendEvents.description: The inaugural RiNo Street Fair celebrates the creative heart of Denver's River North Art District with a full day of activities and community.

weekendEvents.title: Monster Jam Stadium Tour
weekendEvents.description: Monster Jam returns to Empower Field at Mile High for its sixth appearance at the Broncos' stadium, featuring 12,000-pound trucks competing in Freestyle, Skills Challenge, and Racing competitions. A Pit Party on the stadium's south lawn precedes the main event, where fans can see trucks up close, meet drivers, and get autographs.
weekendEvents.location: Empower Field at Mile High, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Opera Colorado: Madama Butterfly
weekendEvents.description: Opera Colorado closes its 43rd season with Puccini's Madama Butterfly, telling the story of Cio-Cio San, a young Japanese geisha whose devotion to an American naval officer leads to devastating heartbreak.

weekendEvents.title: Hadestown
weekendEvents.description: The Tony Award-winning musical intertwines the mythic tales of Orpheus and Eurydice with those of King Hades and Persephone in a hell-raising journey to the underworld and back.
weekendEvents.location: Denver, CO (see venue website for details)

weekendEvents.title: SeriesFest: Season 12
weekendEvents.description: This festival celebrating the art of independent TV series creation showcases the best new pilots from around the globe in an international, juried competition, held in the birthplace of cable television.
weekendEvents.location: Denver, CO (see venue website for details)

weekendEvents.title: Denver Potters Association Spring Show and Sale
weekendEvents.description: The annual Denver Potters show and sale features top artists selling work ranging from ceramics to jewelry, making it a prime opportunity for Mother's Day shopping.
weekendEvents.time: Friday 10 AM–7 PM; Saturday 9 AM–6 PM; Sunday 11 AM–3 PM
weekendEvents.location: Sixth Avenue United Church, 3250 East Sixth Avenue, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Urban Gardens Spring Plant Sale
weekendEvents.description: The annual Denver Urban Gardens Spring Plant Sale returns with three times as many plants as previous years, plus compost, hanging flower baskets, free screenprinting, and SAME Cafe's food truck on site. Proceeds benefit DUG's food access programs.
weekendEvents.location: City Park Greenhouse, 2500 East 23rd Avenue, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Chocolate Festival
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Chocolate Festival returns with chocolate bars, a chocolate fountain, chocolate cake, bonbons, fudge, toffee, a chocolate spa, and jewelry and gifts. Admission is $10; kids 10 and under are free.
weekendEvents.location: Delta by Marriott, I-25 and 120th Avenue, Northglenn

weekendEvents.title: Love Old Buildings Expo
weekendEvents.description: Historic Denver's Love Old Buildings Expo connects old home lovers with craftspeople, preservation specialists, and local experts covering topics from hardwood floors to roofing. The event includes info sessions, hands-on resources, and tours of the 1911 Clayton Campus. Admission is $20.
weekendEvents.location: Clayton Campus Auditorium, 3801 Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Taste of Africa
weekendEvents.description: Presented by A New Dimension of Hope, this evening celebrates the diversity of African culture through authentic dishes from across the continent, live music, and dancing.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Airport Marriott at Gateway Park, 16455 East 40th Circle, Aurora

weekendEvents.title: Wings Over the Rockies Space Race 5K
weekendEvents.description: The inaugural Space Race 5K at Wings Over the Rockies Air & Space Museum welcomes runners, walkers, and leashed pets on an accessible course. Costumes are encouraged, and the post-race celebration features interactive activities, photo opportunities, and a vendor village.
weekendEvents.location: Wings Over the Rockies Air & Space Museum, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Hippie Sabotage with Danny Brown and Kembe X at Red Rocks
weekendEvents.description: Hippie Sabotage headlines Red Rocks Amphitheatre with support from Danny Brown and Kembe X.

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Fire in Strait of Hormuz; Trump Says Ceasefire Remains in Place
topStory.summary:
U.S. and Iranian forces exchanged fire Thursday in the Strait of Hormuz in the most serious breach yet of a month-old ceasefire between the two countries. U.S. Central Command said Iranian forces launched missiles, drones and small boats against three Navy guided-missile destroyers — the USS Truxtun, USS Rafael Peralta and USS Mason — transiting the strait, and that American forces responded by striking Iranian military facilities including missile and drone launch sites, command and control locations, and intelligence and surveillance nodes. The U.S. military said none of the three ships were hit.

President Donald Trump, speaking to reporters during an evening visit to the Reflecting Pool in Washington, said the ceasefire was still holding despite the violence. "They trifled with us today. We blew them away," Trump said, describing the exchange in a separate ABC News interview as a "love tap." Trump also issued a warning on Truth Social, saying the U.S. would respond "a lot harder, and a lot more violently" if Iran did not sign a deal quickly.

Iran offered a sharply different account of events. Iran's top military command alleged the U.S. had first targeted an Iranian oil tanker and another vessel near the strait and conducted aerial strikes on coastal areas including Bandar Khamir, Sirik and Qeshm Island, calling the American actions a ceasefire violation. Iranian state media reported explosions in the strait and described the episode as an "exchange of fire" with the enemy, while also reporting loud noises and defensive fire in western Tehran. Iran's Press TV later reported that the situation near the strait had returned to normal.

The United Arab Emirates added another dimension to the crisis, with its Ministry of Defence announcing Friday that its air defenses were actively engaging with an Iranian missile and drone attack, hours after the U.S. reported thwarting the strikes on its destroyers. Brent crude oil rose to trade around $101 a barrel on news of the attacks, underscoring the economic stakes in a waterway through which hundreds of ships and thousands of mariners remain stranded as Iran maintains control of the route.

The clashes came as diplomatic efforts to end the conflict appeared to be gaining ground. The White House had been reported to be closing in on a 14-point memorandum of understanding with Tehran, circulated through Pakistani mediators, and Iran's foreign ministry had said as recently as Wednesday it was reviewing the latest U.S. proposal. Secretary of State Marco Rubio met Pope Leo XIV at the Vatican on Thursday in a fence-mending visit amid tensions over the war. Regional diplomats warned that Iran risks overplaying its hand, noting that a failure to reach agreement could leave the country under prolonged economic sanctions with no clear path to ending the conflict.
